column2: Invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) in a whole slide image typically presents as irregular, often poorly defined, and infiltrative masses with a mixed pattern of solid and cribriform (cheese-like) architecture. The tumor cells are often pleomorphic, with varying sizes and shapes, and may exhibit atypia such as increased nuclear size, hyperchromasia, and irregular nuclear contours. The stroma surrounding these areas is often abundant and can appear as a "pushing" effect, where the tumor cells displace the normal breast tissue. Nuclei are frequently arranged in single or multiple layers, and mitotic figures can be observed, though they vary in frequency depending on the grade of the tumor. Additionally, IDC may show areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and microcalcifications, which can be seen as small, dense, white spots within the tumor. Capillary-like vascular structures and lymphocytic infiltration are also common features, contributing to the overall heterogeneous appearance of the lesion.

Invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) in a whole slide image typically presents as irregular, often poorly defined, and infiltrative masses with a mixed pattern of solid and cribriform architectural formations. The tumor cells are often pleomorphic, with varying sizes and shapes, and may exhibit atypia characterized by hyperchromatic nuclei, coarse chromatin, and frequent mitotic figures. The stroma surrounding these areas is often rich and can appear as a "pushing" or "infiltrating" front. IDC may also show areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and microcalcifications. The margins of the tumor are usually indistinct, and there may be evidence of invasion into adjacent structures such as blood vessels or lymphatics. Additionally, IDC frequently displays a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio and can have areas of necrotic or cystic degeneration.

Invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) in a whole slide image typically presents as irregular, often poorly defined, and infiltrative masses with a mixed pattern of solid and cribriform (cheese-like) architecture. The tumor cells are often pleomorphic, with varying sizes and shapes, and may exhibit atypia such as increased nuclear size, hyperchromasia, and irregular nuclear contours. The background stroma is frequently infiltrated by tumor cells, leading to a chaotic and disorganized tissue architecture. IDC may also show areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and microcalcifications. The margins of the tumor are usually indistinct, and there can be a surrounding area of reactive stromal changes, including fibrosis and inflammation. Capillary-like vascular structures and lymphocytic infiltration are commonly observed within the tumor mass.

Invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) in a whole slide image typically presents as irregular, often poorly defined, and infiltrative masses with a mixed pattern of solid and cribriform architectural formations. The tumor cells are often pleomorphic, with varying sizes and shapes, and may exhibit atypia characterized by hyperchromatic nuclei, coarse nucleoli, and frequent mitotic figures. The background stroma is often infiltrated by these neoplastic cells, which can form microgrooves or pseudoacinar structures. IDC may also show areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and fibrosis, contributing to a heterogeneous appearance. Additionally, IDC frequently exhibits stromal edema and lymphocytic infiltration, which can be seen as scattered inflammatory cells within the tumor stroma. Capillary-like vascular channels and telangiectasia are common, providing a rich blood supply to the tumor mass.

Invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) in a whole slide image typically presents as irregular, often poorly defined, and infiltrative masses with a mixed pattern of solid and cribriform architectural formations. The tumor cells are often pleomorphic, with varying sizes and shapes, and may exhibit atypia characterized by hyperchromatic nuclei, coarse chromatin, and frequent mitotic figures. The stroma surrounding these areas is often abundant and can appear as a desmoplastic reaction with collagen fibers arranged in a dense, interlaced pattern. IDC may also show areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and microcalcifications. The margins of the tumor are usually indistinct, blending into the surrounding normal breast tissue, which can make it challenging to delineate the tumor boundary. Additionally, IDC may present with areas of comedo necrosis, where the central regions of the tumor are completely necrotic, surrounded by a rim of viable cancer cells.

Invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) in a whole slide image typically presents as irregular, often poorly defined, and infiltrative masses with a mixed pattern of solid and cribriform (cheese-like) architecture. The tumor cells are often pleomorphic, with varying sizes and shapes, and may exhibit atypia such as increased nuclear size, hyperchromasia, and pleomorphism. Nuclei are frequently arranged in single or double rows, and there can be areas of necrosis and hemorrhage. IDC often shows a high mitotic index and may have areas of necrosis and fibrosis. The stroma surrounding the tumor is usually abundant and can appear as a desmoplastic reaction, characterized by dense collagen deposition and fibroblast proliferation. Capillary-like vascular structures are commonly seen within the tumor, and there may be evidence of lymphovascular invasion, which is crucial for staging and prognosis. The margins of the tumor are often ill-defined, and the transition from normal breast tissue to the invasive lesion is gradual rather than abrupt.

IDC may also show microcalcifications, which appear as small, white dots within the tumor or surrounding areas. The borders between the tumor and normal breast tissue are often indistinct, reflecting the invasive nature of the cancer.

The stroma surrounding these areas is often rich and can appear as a desmoplastic reaction with collagen fibers arranged in a dense, interlacing pattern. IDC may also show areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and microcalcifications, which can be seen as small, white, or dark specks within the tumor. The margins of the tumor are usually indistinct, blending into the surrounding normal breast tissue, and the tumor cells may extend into the lymphatics, forming microvascular invasion or lymphovascular spaces.

The background stroma is frequently infiltrated by these neoplastic cells, which can form microgrooves or pseudoacinar structures. IDC may also show areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and fibrosis, contributing to a heterogeneous appearance. Additionally, IDC often displays a high mitotic index and may have areas of necrosis and vascular invasion, which are critical for diagnosis. Capillary-like vascular networks and lymphocytic infiltration around the tumor can also be observed, reflecting the inflammatory response to the cancer.
